steps.action: Belly dance (Arabic: رقص شرقي, romanized: Raqs s with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Belly dance (Arabic: رقص شرقي, romanized: Raqs s with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: A Western-coined exonym, it is also referred to
steps.text: A Western-coined exonym, it is also referred to as Middle Eastern dance or Arabic dance.

steps.action: It has evolved to take many different forms depe
steps.text: It has evolved to take many different forms depending on the country and region, both in costume and dance style, with the styles and costumes of Egypt being the most recognized worldwide due to Egyptian cinema.

steps.action: Belly dancing in its various forms and styles is
steps.text: Belly dancing in its various forms and styles is popular across the globe where it is taught by a trainer of schools of dance.

steps.action: The name first appeared in 1864 in a review of t
steps.text: The name first appeared in 1864 in a review of the Orientalist painting Dance of the Almeh by Jean-Léon Gérôme.

steps.action: The first known use of the term "belly dance" in
steps.text: The first known use of the term "belly dance" in English is found in the writing of Charles James Wills in 1883.

steps.action: Raqs sharqi ('Eastern Dance' or 'Dance of the Or
steps.text: Raqs sharqi ('Eastern Dance' or 'Dance of the Orient') is a broad category of professional forms of the dance, including forms of belly dance popularly known today, such as Raqs Baladi, Sa'idi, Ghawazee, and Awalim.

steps.action: The informal, social form of the dance is known
steps.text: The informal, social form of the dance is known as Raqs Baladi ('Dance of the Country' or 'Folk Dance') in Egyptian Arabic and is considered an indigenous dance.

steps.action: Belly dance is primarily a torso-driven dance, w
steps.text: Belly dance is primarily a torso-driven dance, with an emphasis on articulations of the hips.

steps.action: Unlike many Western dance forms, the focus of th
steps.text: Unlike many Western dance forms, the focus of the dance is on isolations of the torso muscles, rather than on movements of the limbs through space.

steps.action: Many dancers and dance schools have developed th
steps.text: Many dancers and dance schools have developed their own naming schemes, but none of these is universally recognized.

steps.action: The following attempt at categorization reflects
steps.text: The following attempt at categorization reflects the most common naming conventions: Percussive: Staccato movements, most commonly of the hips, used to punctuate the music or accent a beat.

steps.action: Typical movements include horizontal and vertica
steps.text: Typical movements include horizontal and vertical figures of 8 or infinity loops with the hips, horizontal or tilting hip circles, and undulations of the hips and abdomen.

steps.action: In addition to these torso movements, dancers in
steps.text: In addition to these torso movements, dancers in many styles will use level changes, traveling steps, turns, and spins.

steps.action: Later, particularly in the 18th and 19th centuri
steps.text: Later, particularly in the 18th and 19th centuries, European travellers in the Middle East such as Edward Lane and Flaubert wrote extensively of the dancers they saw there, including the Awalim and Ghawazi of Egypt.

steps.action: It's from somewhere in this great, ancient tradi
steps.text: It's from somewhere in this great, ancient tradition of gaiety that the belly dance emerged.

steps.action: " The courtly pleasures of the Umayyad, Abbasid
steps.text: " The courtly pleasures of the Umayyad, Abbasid and Fatimid caliphs included belly dancing, soirée and singing.

steps.action: The belly dancers and singers sent from all part
steps.text: The belly dancers and singers sent from all parts of the vast empire to entertain the wealthy and powerful were as such almost always slaves or prostitutes.

steps.action: During the era of slavery in Egypt, female slave
steps.text: During the era of slavery in Egypt, female slaves were trained in singing and dancing and given as gifts between men.

steps.action: In the Ottoman Empire, belly dance was performed
steps.text: In the Ottoman Empire, belly dance was performed by women and later, by boys, in the sultan's palace.

steps.action: Belly dance remains common in Egypt, where it ha
steps.text: Belly dance remains common in Egypt, where it has two distinct social contexts: as a folk or social dance.

steps.action: As a social dance, belly dance (also called Raqs
steps.text: As a social dance, belly dance (also called Raqs Baladi or Raqs Shaabi in this context) is performed at celebrations and social gatherings by ordinary people (male and female, young and old), in their ordinary clothes.

steps.action: In more conservative or traditional societies, t
steps.text: In more conservative or traditional societies, these events may be gender segregated, with separate parties where men and women dance separately.

steps.action: Historically, professional dance performers were
steps.text: Historically, professional dance performers were the Awalim (primarily musicians and poets), Ghawazi.
